---
title: "Is merger an option?"
---

Cambridge, like many small rural districts in New York, is shrinking. Enrollment
is falling and the population is aging (see [Demographics](demographics.qmd)),
which puts steady pressure on spending, taxes, and reserves (see those chapters).
One structural response New York explicitly allows is **merger** — combining two
or more school districts into one. This chapter explains what merger means in New
York, the financial incentive the state offers for it, what has actually happened
when New York districts have merged, and where Cambridge sits among its neighbors.

Whether merger is right for Cambridge is a decision for the community and its
school board. What follows is the mechanics and the evidence.

## What "merger" means in New York

New York's term is **reorganization** — the act of creating a new school district
where one or more existed before. State Education Law §314 keeps a running
"Master Plan for School District Reorganization" (first drawn up in 1942) so
district boundaries can change as communities' needs and tax bases shift.

There are three main forms:

- **Centralization** — the merging districts dissolve and a **new** school
  district is created covering all of their territory. (This is what most people
  mean by "merger.")
- **Annexation** — one district dissolves and is absorbed into a neighboring
  Central or Union Free district; no new district is created.
- **Annexation by a city school district** — a district outside a city is
  absorbed into the city district, which becomes an "Enlarged City School
  District."

A district can also **"tuition"** students — contract with a neighbor to educate
a grade level — but that is *not* a merger: no new district is formed, and no
reorganization incentive aid is paid.

## How a merger happens

The process takes roughly a year and has several built-in public checkpoints
([NYSED, *School District Reorganization: A Primer*, July 2024](https://www.nysed.gov/sites/default/files/programs/regionalization/school-district-reorganization-a-primer_7.18.2024.pdf)):

1. The boards of the affected districts hold joint meetings and commission a
   **feasibility study** (enrollment, staffing, programs, transportation, and the
   fiscal picture — state aid, spending, and the local tax impact). A state
   **Local Government Efficiency Grant** can offset the study's cost.
2. The boards hold public information meetings.
3. Each district holds a non-binding **"straw vote"** to gauge support.
4. The BOCES District Superintendent reports the straw-vote result to the
   Commissioner of Education; a letter from the Superintendent in support starts
   the formal (statutory) process.
5. The Commissioner issues an order; the statutory vote follows; the earliest a
   new district can begin operating is **July 1**.

## Two routes, two vote thresholds

The single most important practical difference between centralization and
annexation is **how the vote works**:

- **Centralization** requires eligible voters in **each** district to petition for
  a vote, and a **majority in favor at every voting location** is needed to form
  the new district. In other words, *both* communities must say yes.
- **Annexation** is different: the Commissioner's order is **final after 60 days
  unless residents petition for a vote**. A vote is not legally required, and a
  majority in favor at **any one** voting district finalizes the annexation.

So annexation is the lower-bar path — it can be carried by a single district's
yes vote, or even take effect without a vote. That asymmetry shapes which route
real merger discussions tend to take.

## The financial incentive

The state pushes districts toward merger with a large but temporary aid package:

- **Reorganization Incentive Operating Aid (RIOA)** pays the merged district an
  extra **40% of its combined Foundation Aid for the first five years**, then
  drops by four percentage points a year, ending after **14 years**. Since
  2024-25, that percentage is applied to the merged district's Foundation Aid at
  the effective date of the merger; before that it used a frozen 2006-07 base,
  so the 2024 change roughly **tripled** the incentive for a district merging
  today. (A long-dormant statutory limit can reduce the amount for some
  districts; NYSED advises districts to check.)
- **Reorganization Incentive Building Aid** lifts a district's building-aid ratio
  by 30 percentage points (capped at 95%) for new or renovated space, available
  for **10 years**.

For scale, here is what the 14-year RIOA stream looks like applied to
**Cambridge's own Foundation Aid** ($9.55 million in 2025-26) as an illustrative
base. The real amount would be 40% of the **combined** Foundation Aid of whatever
districts merged, so this is a lower bound for a Cambridge-centered merger.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import os, sys
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.insert(0, os.getcwd())
import polars as pl
from plotnine import (
    aes, geom_col, geom_text, ggplot, labs, scale_x_continuous, scale_y_continuous,
    theme,
)
from great_tables import GT, loc, md, style
import comparisons as cc

def _find_repo_root() -> Path:
    cwd = Path.cwd()
    for base in [cwd, *cwd.parents][:5]:
        if (base / "data" / "crosswalks").is_dir():
            return base
    raise FileNotFoundError("Could not find repo root")

REPO_ROOT = _find_repo_root()
PROCESSED = REPO_ROOT / "data" / "processed"

state_aid = pl.read_parquet(PROCESSED / "state_aid.parquet")

# Cambridge's latest Foundation Aid (used as an illustrative RIOA base).
cam_fa = (state_aid.filter((pl.col("district") == "Cambridge") &
                           (pl.col("aid_category") == "Foundation Aid"))
          .sort("year_end").select(pl.col("amount").last()).item())

# RIOA percentage schedule: 40% for 5 years, then -4 pts/yr for 9 years.
RIOA_PCT = [40]*5 + [36, 32, 28, 24, 20, 16, 12, 8, 4]
rioa = pl.DataFrame({"year": list(range(1, 15)), "pct": RIOA_PCT})
rioa = rioa.with_columns((pl.col("pct") / 100 * cam_fa).alias("aid"))
rioa_total = rioa["aid"].sum()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-rioa
#| fig-cap: "Reorganization Incentive Operating Aid over the 14-year eligibility period, shown applied to Cambridge's 2025-26 Foundation Aid ($9.55M) as an illustrative base. The merged district receives 40% of its combined Foundation Aid for five years, then four percentage points less each year. A real Cambridge-centered merger would be calculated on the larger combined base of the merging districts. Source: NYSED Reorganization Primer (2024); RIOA schedule per Education Law §3602."
(
    ggplot(rioa.with_columns((pl.col("aid") / 1e6).alias("aid_m")), aes("year", "aid_m"))
    + geom_col(fill="#C8102E", width=0.7)
    + geom_text(aes(label="pct"), va="bottom", format_string="{}%", size=8, color="#444444")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(1, 15)))
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(0, 4.6), breaks=list(range(0, 5)),
                         labels=lambda lst: [f"${v}M" for v in lst])
    + labs(
        x="Year after merger", y="Incentive aid (illustrative)",
        title="Reorganization incentive aid, 14-year schedule",
        subtitle=f"Applied to Cambridge's Foundation Aid; total ≈ ${rioa_total/1e6:,.1f}M over 14 years",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
    + theme(figure_size=(8, 4.2))
)
```

Two real New York mergers show what that money — and its limits — look like in
practice ([NYSSBA *On Board*, April 2025](https://www.nyssba.org/news/2025/04/04/on-board-online-april-7-2025/officials-in-merged-districts-see-greater-academic-opportunities-for-students/)):

- **Central Valley CSD** (Ilion + Mohawk, Herkimer County, 2013) is on track to
  receive about **$42 million** in incentive aid over its 14 years. It used the
  money to expand offerings (16 AP courses plus 25-30 college-credit courses),
  add sports and extracurriculars, bolster reserves, and hold down the tax levy.
  But it is now in **year 12 of 14** — the incentive is almost gone, and district
  leaders describe hard decisions ahead. (Central Valley got 73.5% of its funding
  from state aid in 2022-23.)
- **Boquet Valley CSD** (Elizabethtown-Lewis + Westport, Essex County, 2019)
  receives **$6 million** over 15 years under the *old* formula — it would have
  gotten **$18 million** under the current one. It expanded electives and AP
  options, but still operates in two aging buildings; a capital project to
  consolidate onto one campus was **defeated by voters**, and the community still
  wrangles over the school's name and colors.

A retired BOCES district superintendent, Robert Guiffreda, has suggested merged
districts split incentive aid roughly into thirds — tax relief, enhanced
programming, and capital needs (more if a new building is required).

## Mergers are rare

For all the incentive, very few New York districts actually merge. The two above
are among the only centralizations in recent decades. Far more common are studies
that go nowhere: roughly **30 districts have studied merger since 2010 without
implementing one** (per the state school business officials' association), and
failed votes dot the record — Scio and Wellsville (2010), Lake Pleasant and Wells
(2011). **Loss of local identity** — team nicknames, mascots, rivalries — is one
of the most frequently cited reasons a merger vote fails.

## What the research says

The broader research on district consolidation (e.g., [a review of the research
on district and school consolidation](https://schoolstatefinance.org/resource-assets/Review-of-Research-on-District-and-School-Consolidation.pdf))
points in a consistent direction on money, a less settled one on academics:

- **Cost savings are real but concentrated among the smallest districts.**
  Combining two small districts can lower per-pupil costs meaningfully; the
  savings shrink as the districts involved get larger, and can be eaten back by
  longer bus rides and higher transportation costs.
- **Academic effects are mixed.** Larger districts can offer more courses and
  programs (as Central Valley and Boquet Valley found), but evidence that
  consolidation *by itself* raises student achievement is weak.
- **The trade-offs are local:** longer travel times, less local control, staff
  disruption, and the loss of a community institution.

In short, the financial case is strongest precisely for the kind of small rural
districts that surround Cambridge.

## Where Cambridge stands

Cambridge is a **mid-size** district in a county of small ones. The table below
places it among its Washington County neighbors (plus Schuylerville and Hoosick
Falls, just across the county line). Cambridge's 741 students and $9.5 million
in Foundation Aid sit in the middle of the pack; **five neighbors are smaller**
by enrollment (Hartford, Fort Edward, Fort Ann, Argyle, Salem) — and the research
above finds consolidation's per-pupil savings are largest precisely when the
districts combining are small.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-districts
#| tbl-cap: "Washington County K-12 districts (plus Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls) by 2024-25 enrollment and 2025-26 Foundation Aid. Foundation aid per pupil = Foundation Aid ÷ K-12 enrollment. Sources: NYSED enrollment (2024-25); NYS state aid (data.ny.gov 9pb8-dg53, 2025-26 enacted)."
group = {**cc.WASHINGTON_K12, "52170104": "Schuylerville", "49050106": "Hoosick Falls"}

enr = (cc.load_panel()
       .filter((pl.col("district_cd").is_in(list(group))) & (pl.col("year_end") == 2025))
       .select(pl.col("district_cd").replace_strict(group).alias("district"),
               "district_cd", "k12_enrollment"))

fa = (state_aid
      .filter((pl.col("aid_category") == "Foundation Aid") & (pl.col("year_end") == 2026))
      .select(pl.col("nysed_district_cd").alias("district_cd"),
              pl.col("amount").alias("foundation_aid")))

tbl = (enr.join(fa, on="district_cd", how="left")
       .with_columns((pl.col("foundation_aid") / pl.col("k12_enrollment")).alias("fa_per_pupil"))
       .sort("k12_enrollment", descending=True)
       .select("district", "k12_enrollment", "foundation_aid", "fa_per_pupil"))

(
    GT(tbl, rowname_col="district")
    .tab_header(title="Cambridge among its neighbors",
                subtitle="2024-25 enrollment and 2025-26 Foundation Aid")
    .fmt_number(columns="k12_enrollment", decimals=0)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["foundation_aid", "fa_per_pupil"], decimals=0)
    .cols_label(k12_enrollment="K-12 enrollment",
                foundation_aid="Foundation Aid",
                fa_per_pupil="Foundation Aid per pupil")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == "Cambridge"),
                   loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == "Cambridge")],
    )
    .tab_source_note(md("**Source:** NYSED Enrollment database (2024-25 K-12 enrollment); "
                        "NYS state aid (data.ny.gov, 2025-26 enacted Foundation Aid)."))
)
```

Cambridge's own **Reorganization Incentive Operating Aid is $0** — it has never
merged. So the incentive chart above is a look at what the state *would* pay, not
what it pays now.

The pressure behind any merger discussion here is the same one running through
this book: a declining, aging population means fewer students and a thinner local
tax base, while the fixed cost of running a school barely shrinks (see
[Spending](spending.qmd) and [Demographics](demographics.qmd)). Merger is one way
to spread those fixed costs across more students — temporarily subsidized by the
state, and not without cost to the community.

*Sources:*

- NYSED, *School District Reorganization: A Primer* (July 2024) — process, vote rules, RIOA and Building Aid formulas.
- [NYSSBA *On Board*, "Officials in merged districts see greater academic opportunities," April 7, 2025](https://www.nyssba.org/news/2025/04/04/on-board-online-april-7-2025/officials-in-merged-districts-see-greater-academic-opportunities-for-students/) — Central Valley and Boquet Valley case studies.
- [Review of the Research on District & School Consolidation](https://schoolstatefinance.org/resource-assets/Review-of-Research-on-District-and-School-Consolidation.pdf) — evidence on costs and outcomes.
- NYS state school aid, data.ny.gov dataset `9pb8-dg53` — Foundation Aid by district.
